reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All three module names (atlasMongo, upstashRedis, ecsBlueGreen) are correctly sourced from the implementation file and linked to their resource pages.
The interactive flow is accurately described — select module, initialize registry, load private types, register newest version — matching the source code exactly.
Multiple realistic examples cover the common variations: different region, named AWS profile, debug log level. Each has its own fenced block with no explanatory comments inside.
The per-region behavior callout in <Info> is grounded and practically important — CloudFormation private types are region-scoped.
Related commands (deploy, cf:rollback, compile-template) are well-chosen and immediately useful to someone reaching this page.
Correct use of <CliCommandsApiReference> with accurate sortedArgs matching the universalArgs set plus region.
problems: The 'Arguments reference' section appears after 'Examples'. The CLI section instructions explicitly order: intro → usage example → auto-generated reference → examples → related commands. Having the flags table after the examples breaks that rhythm and makes the page harder to scan for flag-lookup use cases.

The 'When to use' H2 section partially duplicates the intro paragraph. The CLI section instructions say the intro paragraph should state what the command does and when you'd use it — a separate 'When to use' H2 creates redundancy rather than additional depth, adding scroll distance for a narrow-scope command.
optionalImprovements: Move the 'Arguments reference' section to immediately follow the Usage section (and its example), placing it before 'When to use' and 'Examples' to match the CLI page rhythm from the section instructions.

Fold the 'When to use' bullet list into the intro paragraph or a short paragraph below it, then remove the H2. The command is narrow enough that a single well-written intro paragraph can carry this guidance without a dedicated section.

Consider adding a one-sentence note about what a registration failure typically looks like (e.g., a CloudFormation stack event error on third-party resource creation) so a reader landing here from a broken deploy has confirmation they're in the right place. The source doesn't surface error messages explicitly, so keep this cautious and brief.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Opening paragraph is accurate, self-contained, and grounded in the source — correctly describes the command's purpose and the interactive module-selection flow.
All three module names (atlasMongo, upstashRedis, ecsBlueGreen) are correctly sourced from the allowedModules array in src/commands/cf-module-update/index.ts and linked to their resource pages.
Correct use of <CliCommandsApiReference command="cf-module:update" sortedArgs={[...]} /> — no hand-authored flags table, component is wired properly.
Each example has its own fenced block with a prose sentence above it — no multiple commands packed into one block.
The multi-region Info callout is a useful practical note grounded in AWS regional scoping of CloudFormation type registrations and the fact that --region is a required argument.
Related commands are relevant and link to the three most likely companion commands a user would reach for.
problems: Section order deviates from the CLI page instructions: the prescribed rhythm is 'opening paragraph → usage example → auto-generated reference → Examples → Related commands'. The draft places 'When to use' after Usage and then puts the Arguments reference AFTER Examples. A reader who came to look up a flag must scroll past the entire Examples section to find the flags table. This is the most actionable structural issue.

The 'When to use' section, while useful, is not prescribed for CLI reference pages in the section instructions. Its third bullet ('you want to proactively update module types before deploying') is logically fine but slightly circular — it describes wanting to use the command as a reason to use the command. A tighter rewrite or folding this content into the opening paragraph would improve the page.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder sections to match the CLI page rhythm: (1) opening paragraph, (2) Usage example, (3) Arguments reference (<CliCommandsApiReference>), (4) Examples with non-obvious patterns, (5) Related commands. Move the Arguments reference section above the Examples section. The 'When to use' block can either be removed or folded into the opening paragraph as a second sentence — it should not sit between Usage and the flags table.
optionalImprovements: Tighten the third 'When to use' bullet — 'you want to proactively update before deploying after Stacktape announces a new module version' is the real scenario; rephrase to make that concrete rather than circular.

Consider noting that the command requires an active Stacktape login (loadUserCredentials is called in the source after the module selection prompt) — users who haven't run `stacktape login` will hit a credentials error mid-flow.

The description of ecsBlueGreen as 'the CloudFormation type supporting ECS Blue/Green deployments' is accurate but brief. One additional clause clarifying it is used for zero-downtime container deployments would help readers decide whether they need it.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph clearly states what `cf-module:update` does, names the affected module families, and gives a plausible use case.
Usage examples are copyable, one command per shell block, and include the required `--region` flag.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="cf-module:update" ... />` component instead of hand-authoring the arguments table.
The interactive prompt behavior and the three allowed module values are grounded in `src/commands/cf-module-update/index.ts`.
problems: The page order does not follow the CLI section guidance: the generated arguments reference should come immediately after the usage example, with non-obvious examples after it.

The claim that Stacktape uses these modules to integrate "third-party services — MongoDB Atlas, Upstash Redis, and ECS Blue/Green deployments" is imprecise because ECS Blue/Green is not a third-party service in the same sense as MongoDB Atlas or Upstash Redis.

The sentence "Run this command when a third-party API changes" overgeneralizes the source. The command updates CloudFormation private types; the source mentions third-party API changes, but the safer user-facing trigger is a deploy or registration issue involving one of the supported module types.

The "When to use" section mentions proactive updates after Stacktape announces a new module version, but the provided source does not show announcements or a release-notification flow for this command.
The page says Stacktape "loads the current private types"; the runtime method name is `loadPrivateTypesAndPackages`, so the wording is close but slightly narrower than the source behavior.

The page has no FAQ. This is less severe for a CLI reference page, but a short FAQ could improve answer-engine extraction for common questions like whether the command is regional, interactive, and which modules are supported.
mandatoryFixes: Move `<CliCommandsApiReference command="cf-module:update" ... />` so it appears after the Usage section, before Examples, matching the CLI page structure guidance.

Replace the imprecise third-party-services wording with a scoped statement such as: "Stacktape currently offers updates for `atlasMongo`, `upstashRedis`, and `ecsBlueGreen` CloudFormation private types."
Remove or reword the unsupported proactive-announcement bullet unless a source file for Stacktape announcements is provided.
optionalImprovements: Add a short note that the command prompts for one module and does not expose a documented flag for selecting the module non-interactively, based on the runtime prompt in `commandCfModuleUpdate`.
Rename "Arguments reference" to "Arguments" or "Reference" for a shorter H2.
Add 4-6 concise FAQ entries if the CLI docs convention allows it, covering supported modules, regional scope, interactivity, credentials, and when to prefer `deploy` versus `cf-module:update`.
Clarify that `loadUserCredentials()` runs after module selection, so the command requires valid Stacktape/AWS credentials even though only `--region` is listed as a required command argument.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided CLI command definition and cf-module:update implementation. I found two issues: one unsupported framing of ECS Blue/Green as a third-party service, and one over-specific troubleshooting claim about deploy-time errors that is not shown by the provided sources.

issues.statement: “Stacktape uses these modules to integrate third-party services — MongoDB Atlas, Upstash Redis, and ECS Blue/Green deployments — into your stack.”
issues.reason: The command description supports CloudFormation infrastructure modules being used for third-party services, and the implementation lists `atlasMongo`, `upstashRedis`, and `ecsBlueGreen` as selectable modules. However, the source does not support describing ECS Blue/Green deployments as a third-party service. That wording could mislead readers about what the ECS Blue/Green module is for.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence: “Stacktape uses these modules for integrations such as MongoDB Atlas and Upstash Redis, and for ECS Blue/Green deployment support.”

evidence.quote: AWS CloudFormation infrastructure modules are used to integrate third-party services into your stack. If a third-party API changes, use this command to update the modules in your account to the latest version.

issues.statement: “You receive errors related to CloudFormation private type registration during deployment of MongoDB Atlas, Upstash Redis, or ECS Blue/Green resources.”
issues.reason: The command implementation shows that `cf-module:update` registers the newest available private type and can fail while registering a module, but the provided sources do not show deployment error patterns or state that deployment errors related to private type registration should be handled this way. The statement is plausible but over-specific for the available source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously: “Use it when Stacktape support or a deployment error indicates that one of these CloudFormation module private types needs to be updated.”

evidence.quote: If a third-party API changes, use this command to update the modules in your account to the latest version.

positiveFindings: The command name, required `--region` argument, and documented global arguments match the CLI command definition.
The interactive module picker and selectable module names are grounded in `src/commands/cf-module-update/index.ts`.
The CloudFormation registry initialization, private type loading, and newest-version registration flow matches the command implementation.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided navigation index.
The draft uses one command per shell block and does not include forbidden Stacktape configuration examples.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the CLI command definition and implementation. The main problems are unsupported usage guidance around proactive updates and a few prose claims that over-specify what the source proves about specific modules and credential/profile behavior.

issues.statement: "You want to proactively update module types before deploying, for example after Stacktape announces a new module version."
issues.reason: The provided sources say to use the command when a third-party API changes and modules need the latest version. They do not mention Stacktape announcements or proactive pre-deploy maintenance as a documented use case.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this bullet to the sourced condition: run the command when a third-party API change requires a newer CloudFormation infrastructure module version, or when instructed by Stacktape support/docs if that instruction is sourced elsewhere.

evidence.quote: If a third-party API changes, use this command to update the modules in your account to the latest version.

issues.statement: "The three available modules are: atlasMongo — the CloudFormation type backing MongoDB Atlas clusters; upstashRedis — the CloudFormation type backing Upstash Redis resources; ecsBlueGreen — the CloudFormation type supporting ECS Blue/Green deployments."
issues.reason: The implementation proves only the selectable module identifiers. The source does not describe what each module backs, and the command description only generally says CloudFormation infrastructure modules integrate third-party services into a stack.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the identifiers and describe them cautiously as the selectable module types: `atlasMongo`, `upstashRedis`, and `ecsBlueGreen`. If keeping the explanatory labels, phrase them as inferred from the module names rather than as proven backing relationships.

evidence.quote: AWS CloudFormation infrastructure modules are used to integrate third-party services into your stack.

issues.statement: "Use a specific AWS profile for credentials." followed by `stacktape cf-module:update --region eu-west-1 --profile my-aws-profile`.
issues.reason: The command accepts the universal `profile` argument, but the cf-module:update implementation explicitly calls `loadUserCredentials()`. The provided sources do not show that `--profile` is used as AWS credentials for this command.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove this example or phrase it only as an accepted CLI argument from the generated reference, without claiming how the credentials are resolved unless the credential-loading source is provided.

issues.statement: "You do not need to run this command regularly. Module types only need updating when the upstream provider's API changes in a way that requires a new CloudFormation type version."
issues.reason: The second sentence is grounded by the command description, but the stronger operational claim that users do not need to run it regularly is not stated directly in the provided source. It is likely reasonable guidance, but it should be framed as a practical implication rather than a documented guarantee.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to: "In normal use, run this command only when a third-party API change requires the module in your account to be updated."

positiveFindings: The required `--region` usage is correct for `cf-module:update`.
The draft correctly states that the command is interactive and prompts the user to choose one of `atlasMongo`, `upstashRedis`, or `ecsBlueGreen`.
The `CliCommandsApiReference` uses the correct command name, `cf-module:update`.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly complete for a narrow CLI reference page. The command has only one required command-specific argument, `--region`, and the page uses the required auto-generated CLI reference component. I found one medium issue where the prose overstates the supported modules as third-party integrations even though one allowed module is ECS Blue/Green.

issues.statement: “Stacktape uses these modules to integrate third-party services — MongoDB Atlas, Upstash Redis, and ECS Blue/Green deployments — into your stack.”
issues.reason: The source supports the three selectable module names, but describing all three as third-party service integrations is misleading because `ecsBlueGreen` is not a third-party provider integration in the same sense as MongoDB Atlas or Upstash Redis. The command source only names the allowed modules; it does not group all three under third-party services.
issues.suggestedFix: Split the wording: “Stacktape uses these modules for CloudFormation-backed integrations such as MongoDB Atlas and Upstash Redis, and for the ECS Blue/Green module used by blue/green deployments.”

evidence.quote: AWS CloudFormation infrastructure modules are used to integrate third-party services into your stack. If a third-party API changes, use this command to update the modules in your account to the latest version.

positiveFindings: The page documents exactly one CLI command and includes the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="cf-module:update" ... />` component.
The usage example includes the required `--region` flag, matching `requiredArgs: ['region']`.
The draft correctly states that the command is interactive and that the user selects from `atlasMongo`, `upstashRedis`, and `ecsBlueGreen`, matching the implementation prompt.
The Examples section keeps one shell command per fenced `bash` block and avoids copy-buffer comments.
